title: Docker
objectives: Understand Docker via a real-life analogy
Distinguish images, containers, layers, volumes, networks
Use docker-compose for local multi-service dev

sections.markdown:
**Think of Docker as shipping containers for software.**

- Before shipping containers (1950s), moving cargo was chaos — every ship was custom-loaded, every dock had its own cranes, every truck had its own dimensions. A crate that fit in one truck was too big for another. Delays were massive; theft was rampant; unloading took days.
- **The standardized shipping container** changed everything. Same box, same corners, same locks. Any crane, any truck, any ship can move it. What's INSIDE the container is your business — cars, refrigerators, tea. The container's OUTSIDE is standard.
- **Docker containers** do the same for software. Your app + all its dependencies (Java 21, specific curl version, specific timezone data, config files, everything except the OS kernel) are packaged into a standard IMAGE. Any machine with Docker installed can run any image identically — laptop, staging server, production Kubernetes.

**Concretely:** Docker packages your app into an IMAGE (a layered filesystem + start command). You run an image to create a CONTAINER (a process isolated by Linux namespaces + cgroups). The container thinks it has its own filesystem, network, PID space — but shares the host kernel (unlike a VM). Result: startup in milliseconds, image sizes in MB, thousands of containers per host.

**Why it dominates:** every non-trivial backend in the last 10 years ships as a Docker image. Kubernetes assumes it. CI/CD pipelines build/push it. 'Works on my machine' died the day teams started using Docker. **Learn Docker deeply; every ops-adjacent role expects it.**

**Docker** is a platform for building, shipping, and running applications as **containers** — lightweight, isolated processes sharing the host OS kernel. Built on Linux **namespaces** (process/network/filesystem isolation) and **cgroups** (CPU/memory limits). Core concepts: **image** (immutable layered filesystem template), **container** (running instance of an image), **Dockerfile** (recipe for building an image), **registry** (image storage — Docker Hub, ECR, GHCR), **volume** (persistent data outside the container's writable layer), **network** (virtual networks between containers). Introduced 2013; standard for backend deployment since ~2016.
## The four things you'll use every day
## Sample program — production-grade multi-stage Dockerfile

sections.code:
# Dockerfile — multi-stage build for a Spring Boot app.
# Multi-stage separates the BUILD environment (heavy — JDK + Gradle + sources)
# from the RUNTIME (small — JRE only). Final image is 10x smaller.

# ---------- STAGE 1: build ----------
FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jdk-alpine AS build
WORKDIR /src

# Copy dependency manifests first — layer caching means Gradle downloads only re-run
# if these change. Copying sources later ensures code edits don't invalidate deps.
COPY gradlew settings.gradle build.gradle ./
COPY gradle ./gradle
RUN ./gradlew --no-daemon dependencies || true

COPY src ./src
RUN ./gradlew --no-daemon bootJar

# ---------- STAGE 2: runtime ----------
FROM eclipse-temurin:21-jre-alpine

# Non-root user — never run as root in prod containers.
RUN addgroup -S app && adduser -S app -G app
WORKDIR /app
USER app

# Copy JUST the fat jar from the build stage.
COPY --from=build --chown=app:app /src/build/libs/*.jar app.jar

# Environment: JVM heap sensible for containers.
ENV JAVA_OPTS="-XX:MaxRAMPercentage=75.0 -XX:+UseZGC"

# Health check — Kubernetes and monitors use this.
HEALTHCHECK --interval=30s --timeout=3s --start-period=30s --retries=3 \
  CMD wget -q --spider http://localhost:8080/actuator/health || exit 1

EXPOSE 8080

# ENTRYPOINT with exec form — signals (SIGTERM) forwarded correctly to Java.
ENTRYPOINT ["sh", "-c", "java $JAVA_OPTS -jar app.jar"]

# Result:
#   docker build -t myapp:1.0 .
#   docker image ls  → ~180MB (vs ~800MB with JDK)
#   docker run -p 8080:8080 --rm myapp:1.0

sections.markdown: ## docker-compose — the local-dev workhorse

sections.code:
# compose.yaml — multi-container app for local development.
name: myapp-dev

services:
  app:
    build: .
    ports:
      - "8080:8080"
    environment:
      SPRING_DATASOURCE_URL: jdbc:postgresql://postgres:5432/myapp
      SPRING_DATASOURCE_USERNAME: myapp
      SPRING_DATASOURCE_PASSWORD: dev
      SPRING_REDIS_HOST: redis
    depends_on:
      postgres:
        condition: service_healthy
      redis:
        condition: service_healthy

  postgres:
    image: postgres:16-alpine
    environment:
      POSTGRES_DB: myapp
      POSTGRES_USER: myapp
      POSTGRES_PASSWORD: dev
    ports:
      - "5432:5432"
    volumes:
      - pgdata:/var/lib/postgresql/data     # named volume — survives compose down
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD-SHELL", "pg_isready -U myapp"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 5

  redis:
    image: redis:7-alpine
    ports:
      - "6379:6379"
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "redis-cli", "ping"]
      interval: 5s
      timeout: 3s
      retries: 5

volumes:
  pgdata:

# Run:
#   docker compose up -d          # start everything
#   docker compose logs -f app    # tail app logs
#   docker compose exec postgres psql -U myapp    # shell into postgres
#   docker compose down           # stop; keep volumes
#   docker compose down -v        # stop; DELETE volumes (fresh start)

sections.markdown: ## Layer caching — the biggest perf win to understand

sections.code:
Dockerfile builds are LAYERED. Each instruction (COPY, RUN, etc.) creates a layer.
Docker CACHES layers. If a layer's input hasn't changed, Docker reuses the cached
layer instead of re-running the command.

BAD ordering — one line change invalidates everything:

  COPY . .                      # entire source tree — changes every edit
  RUN npm install                # re-runs on EVERY code change (slow)

GOOD ordering — copy files that change RARELY first:

  COPY package.json package-lock.json ./     # rarely changes
  RUN npm install                             # cached unless package files change
  COPY . .                                     # source last

Rule: put SLOW, RARELY-CHANGING steps at the TOP.
Put FAST, FREQUENTLY-CHANGING steps at the BOTTOM.

Build time drops from '3 minutes every edit' to '3 seconds every edit'.

sections.markdown: ## Common mistakes
**Mistake 1: Running as root.** Default Docker containers run as UID 0. If someone escapes the container, they're root on the host. Always `USER app` (non-root) in your Dockerfile.
**Mistake 2: `latest` tag in production.** Which version is running? Impossible to say. Pin explicit versions: `myapp:1.2.3`, not `myapp:latest`.
**Mistake 3: No multi-stage builds.** Shipping a 1GB image with the entire JDK + Gradle. Runtime doesn't need those — use a small runtime base.
**Mistake 4: Storing state in the container.** Containers are EPHEMERAL. Any writes to the container's writable layer die on restart. Use volumes / external DBs for persistence.
**Mistake 5: Secrets baked into images.** `ENV DB_PASSWORD=hunter2` — anyone who pulls your image can `docker inspect` and see it. Inject at runtime (env vars, Docker secrets, K8s Secrets).
**Mistake 6: No `.dockerignore`.** Every build ships your `.git/`, `node_modules/`, `target/`, `.env`. Big context, slow builds, potential secret leaks. Write `.dockerignore` like `.gitignore`.
**Mistake 7: Ignoring image size.** 1.5GB base image = slow deploys + big attack surface. Use `-alpine` or distroless variants; strip debug tools.
## Best practices
**1. Multi-stage builds.** JDK for build, JRE (or distroless) for runtime. 10x smaller.
**2. Non-root user.** `USER app` (not root).
**3. Pin explicit versions.** `alpine:3.20` not `alpine:latest`. Reproducible builds.
**4. `.dockerignore` file.** Exclude `.git/`, `node_modules/`, `build/`, secrets.
**5. Layer caching order.** Rarely-changing files first (`package.json`), then `RUN install`, then source.
**6. HEALTHCHECK.** K8s + monitors use it to know if the app is truly ready.
**7. Signal handling.** Use `exec` form of ENTRYPOINT so SIGTERM reaches your app. `["java", "-jar", "app.jar"]`.
**8. Scan images.** Trivy, Snyk, Docker Scout — catch CVEs before deploy.
## Practice programs
**Practice 1 (Easy) — Hello world image.** `FROM alpine` + `CMD echo hello`. Build, run, verify output.
**Practice 2 (Medium) — Multi-stage Java build.** Dockerfile that builds a Spring Boot jar in stage 1 and runs it on JRE in stage 2. Measure image sizes before/after.
**Practice 3 (Medium) — docker-compose stack.** App + Postgres + Redis with healthchecks; verify `depends_on: service_healthy` works.
**Practice 4 (Hard) — Named volume for Postgres.** Add data, restart, verify persistence. Use `docker compose down -v` to wipe and confirm fresh state.
**Practice 5 (Hard) — Optimize layer caching.** Measure build time for a code-only change with bad vs good Dockerfile ordering.
**Practice 6 (Expert) — Distroless image + Trivy scan.** Move to `gcr.io/distroless/java-base`; scan with Trivy; fix any CVEs found.
## Interview Q&A — with full answers you can quote

**Q1: What is Docker and how is it different from a VM?**

**A:** Docker is a platform for packaging apps as **containers** — lightweight isolated processes sharing the host OS KERNEL. **Difference from VMs:** a VM runs a full guest OS (kernel + userland) on top of a hypervisor — heavy (GBs), slow to start (minutes), full isolation. A container is just a PROCESS with its own filesystem, network, and PID namespace, sharing the host kernel — lightweight (MBs), starts in ms, 'strong-enough' isolation. **Trade-off:** VMs give hardware-level isolation (safer for untrusted multi-tenant); containers give speed + density (thousands per host). **In practice:** modern backends run in containers; VMs are used for infrastructure (host the container engine) or when you need full OS isolation.

**Q2: What is a Docker image vs a container?**

**A:** An **image** is an IMMUTABLE, layered filesystem template plus metadata (default command, exposed ports, environment). Think 'blueprint' or 'installer'. A **container** is a RUNNING INSTANCE of an image — a live process with its own writable layer on top. **Analogy:** image is like a class; container is like an object. You can create many containers from one image. Containers share the underlying image's layers (efficient — the JVM base layer is shared across every Spring Boot container on the host). **Lifecycle:** `docker build` produces images; `docker run` creates + starts containers; `docker ps` lists running; `docker exec` runs a command inside; `docker stop`/`rm` cleans up.

**Q3: What is a multi-stage Dockerfile?**

**A:** A Dockerfile with multiple `FROM` statements — each starts a new BUILD STAGE. You typically use a HEAVY stage (JDK + Gradle) to compile, then COPY the artifact into a LIGHTER stage (JRE only, or distroless). The final image contains ONLY the runtime stage — build tools are discarded. **Why:** dramatically smaller images (a Spring Boot image drops from ~800MB to ~180MB). Smaller images = faster pull/push, smaller attack surface, faster K8s scheduling. **Syntax:** `FROM ... AS build`, then `FROM ... COPY --from=build /path/to/artifact /app`. Standard practice for any production Dockerfile since ~2017.

**Q4: How does Docker layer caching work?**

**A:** Each Dockerfile instruction (`COPY`, `RUN`, `ENV`, etc.) produces a LAYER. Docker caches layers based on the instruction's INPUTS. When building, Docker checks: for each layer, are the inputs (previous layer hash + instruction + files copied) the same as last time? If yes → REUSE the cached layer. If no → re-run this instruction AND every layer after it. **Optimization:** put SLOW + RARELY-CHANGING steps FIRST. Example: `COPY package.json` + `RUN npm install` BEFORE `COPY .`. If only source changes, `npm install` layer is cached — build takes seconds instead of minutes. **Killer mistake:** `COPY . .` before `RUN npm install` invalidates dependencies on every code edit. This ONE ordering rule saves hours per week.

**Q5: What's the difference between a volume and a bind mount?**

**A:** Both persist data outside the container's writable layer. **Volume** — managed by Docker (`docker volume create data`; `-v data:/app/data`). Stored in Docker's storage location (`/var/lib/docker/volumes/`). Preferred for production. Portable across environments. **Bind mount** — maps a HOST path to a container path (`-v /home/me/code:/app`). Useful for dev (edit code on host, container sees changes live) or for shared filesystems. Less portable — host path must exist. **Rule:** volumes for persistent app data (Postgres, uploaded files); bind mounts for dev-only source editing. **Anonymous volumes** (`-v /app/data` with no name) are created on the fly and orphaned when the container is removed — mostly a mistake.

**Q6: How do you keep secrets out of Docker images?**

**A:** **Never bake them in.** `ENV DB_PASSWORD=hunter2` or `COPY .env /app/` is a leak. Anyone who pulls your image can `docker inspect` or extract layers and find them. **Options:** **(1) Environment variables at runtime** — `docker run -e DB_PASSWORD=$SECRET` with the secret from your deployment platform (K8s Secrets, HashiCorp Vault). **(2) Docker secrets** (Swarm) — mounted as tmpfs files at `/run/secrets/`. **(3) K8s Secrets** — mounted as env or files at container start. **(4) External secret manager** — app fetches secrets from Vault/AWS Secrets Manager at startup. **(5) BuildKit `--secret` mount** — a secret available ONLY during `RUN` steps and NOT persisted in image layers. **Rule:** the image is public information. Never put secrets there.

**Q7: What's docker-compose and when do you use it?**

**A:** `docker-compose` (now `docker compose` — built into Docker CLI since 2022) is a tool for defining and running MULTI-CONTAINER apps declaratively via a YAML file. You define services (app, database, cache, message broker), their networks, volumes, environment. `docker compose up -d` starts them all with correct dependencies. **When:** primarily for LOCAL DEVELOPMENT (spin up your app + Postgres + Redis with one command) and CI (integration tests against real dependencies). **Also for:** small deployments (single-host, 5-10 containers). **When NOT:** production at scale — use Kubernetes. Compose has no cluster support, no rolling deploys, no autoscaling. Rule: `docker compose up` for laptop; K8s for prod.

## Real-world usage

- **Every non-trivial backend** ships as a Docker image.
- **Kubernetes** assumes container images (OCI format = Docker + others).
- **CI/CD** builds images on every commit; pushes to a registry (Docker Hub, GHCR, ECR, GCR).
- **Local dev** — `docker compose up` for the whole app stack.
- **Testing** — Testcontainers spins up real Postgres/Kafka/Redis for integration tests.
- **Alternatives** — Podman (rootless, daemonless), containerd (Docker's underlying runtime), OCI standards mean images work across all of them.

## Advanced notes

- **BuildKit** — the modern Docker build backend; enables `--secret`, `--ssh`, parallel stages, faster caching.
- **`.dockerignore`** — exclude files from build context (`.git`, `node_modules`, build artifacts).
- **Distroless images** — Google's minimal images with NO shell, NO package manager. Just your app + runtime.
- **Non-root by default** — many modern base images (`node:22-alpine`) now include a `node` user; use it.
- **`--platform`** — build for arm64 or amd64 explicitly; matters for M-series Macs.
- **Multi-arch images** — one tag, multiple architectures; `docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64,linux/arm64`.
- **`docker system prune`** — reclaim disk from stopped containers, unused images, dangling layers.
- **Registry proxies** — pull-through cache for offline / slow-network builds.
- **Container escape CVEs** — patch your Docker engine + runc regularly.
- **Rootless Docker** — run the Docker daemon as non-root user; stronger isolation.
- **OCI runtime spec** — the standard behind `runc`, `crun`, etc.
- **Windows containers** — Windows-native containers exist; different than Linux containers.

flashcards.back: Container shares host KERNEL (lightweight, ms startup, MB size). VM has full guest OS (heavy, minutes, GB).
Image = immutable template. Container = running instance of an image.
Multiple FROM stages; final stage COPYs artifact from build stage. Result: 10x smaller image.
Put SLOW + RARELY-CHANGING steps at the top; FAST + FREQUENTLY-CHANGING at the bottom.
Volume = Docker-managed (production). Bind mount = host path (dev, source editing).
NEVER bake them in. Inject at runtime via env vars, Docker/K8s Secrets, or Vault.

quiz.explain: Namespaces + cgroups, not a full guest OS.

options: Slow rarely-changing FIRST; fast frequently-changing LAST
Smaller image — discard build tools in final stage
quiz.explain: Ship only what you need to run.

options: Injected at runtime (env, Docker Secrets, K8s Secrets, Vault)

challenges.text: Write a Dockerfile that runs `nginx` serving a static HTML page.
Build + tag + push an image to Docker Hub.
Multi-stage Spring Boot Dockerfile; compare image sizes.
compose.yaml with app + Postgres + Redis + healthchecks.
Optimize layer caching; measure build time delta.
Non-root user, distroless image, Trivy scan clean.
Multi-arch buildx image (amd64 + arm64) pushed to a registry.
